▶ Agent PMO

  • How It Works
  • Features
  • Get Started
GitHub
What's Inside

Every feature exists for
one reason: fewer blockers.

Agent PMO is two components — the PMO Dashboard and Repo Standards Enforcement — that together let one developer manage an organisation's worth of output.

📡
Component 1

PMO Dashboard

Your control panel. An F# script scans every repo under ~/Documents/Code/, gathers status from the git index and GitHub API, and generates a self-contained HTML report — refreshed every 3 minutes via launchd. No server. No login. No cloud.

Per-repo it collects:

  • Uncommitted file count
  • Current branch name and push/pull status
  • Last commit date and message
  • Open PRs — with a fallback to any open PR if branch differs
  • CI status via gh CLI
  • Latest GitHub release tag

You're not context-switching into each repo to figure out what's happening. The dashboard tells you. You decide where to direct attention. That's minimal cognitive load.

PR detection is robust: the lookup first checks for a PR matching the current branch. If none is found, it falls back to any open PR in the repo. The "PR Branch" column shows which branch the PR is actually from.
~ repo-report.html 
✓ app-backend
  branch: main
  PR #47 · CI green
  last: 2h ago

⚠ data-pipeline
  branch: feat/ingest
  PR #12 · CI running
  3 uncommitted

✓ mobile-client
  branch: feat/auth
  PR #8 · CI green
  awaiting review

↻ api-gateway
  branch: main
  no PR · CI pending
  agent: linting
📐
Component 2

Repo Standards Enforcement

Agents can't navigate chaos. If every project has a different structure, different scripts, different CI config — every project requires hand-holding. That hand-holding is what keeps you serialised.

Consistency solves this. The enforcement skill applies portfolio-wide templates to any repo:

  • Standardised Makefile with identical targets across all repos
  • Unified CI pipeline templates (GitHub Actions)
  • Linter configs for every supported language
  • Coverage config with monotonically-increasing thresholds
  • Dev container configs for consistent environments
  • Language-specific .gitignore templates
  • CLAUDE.md injected into every repo so agents know the rules

Drop an agent into any project and it already knows how to run, test, and ship — no setup, no babysitting. That's what makes twenty projects manageable instead of twenty separate headaches.

~ make (any repo) 
# Same targets, every repo:
$ make build   # compile
$ make test    # test + coverage
$ make lint    # lint + typecheck
$ make fmt     # format code
$ make check   # lint + test
$ make ci      # full CI pipeline

✓ Agent knows how to work in any repo
✓ Zero project-specific setup
🔒
Non-negotiable

Automated Quality Gates

Agents don't hand you rough drafts. They fight through quality gates the entire way. There is no soft-fail mode.

  • Lint — format violations tank the pipeline
  • Type check — strict mode, no any escapes
  • Code format — enforced by CI, not just recommended
  • Unit tests — must pass; no skipping
  • Integration tests — end-to-end verification
  • Coverage thresholds — monotonically increasing, never regress
  • CI pipeline — GitHub Actions gate before merge

By the time code reaches you, it has passed every automated check. You're the final gate — reviewing for intent, architecture, and edge cases. Not checking whether the tests pass.

~ CI pipeline output 
✓ lint          0 violations
✓ typecheck     0 errors
✓ format        clean
✓ unit tests    142 passed
✓ integration   28 passed
✓ coverage      87% ≥ 85%

✓ All gates passed — ready for review
🌿
Parallelism

Git Worktrees

Got a monolith or multi-service codebase that can't be split up? Git worktrees solve this. Each agent gets its own worktree — an isolated branch at a different filesystem path. Same repo. No conflicts. No stepping on each other.

A monolith becomes multiple parallel workstreams. Three agents can work on three different features of the same codebase simultaneously, each with a clean working tree, each on its own branch, each running its own tests.

  • Same repo, different paths — no file conflicts
  • Each worktree has its own branch and index
  • Agents can build and test independently
  • TMC coordinates when branches need to merge
~ git worktree list 
$ git worktree list

~/Code/myapp          main
~/Code/myapp-feat-auth feat/auth
~/Code/myapp-fix-api   fix/api-timeout
~/Code/myapp-coverage  chore/coverage

3 agents · 3 branches · 0 conflicts
🎭
Orchestration

TMC — Too Many Cooks

When the TMC server is available, it acts as the conductor for cross-project agent orchestration. TMC dispatches work, manages dependencies, and avoids conflicts across agents and projects.

TMC sequences, not just parallelises. If a feature branch needs passing tests before a PR opens, TMC enforces that. If two projects share a library, TMC knows.

Agent protocol when TMC is available:

  • Register immediately: descriptive name, intent, files to touch
  • Lock files before editing — never edit a locked file
  • Broadcast plans before starting work
  • Check messages every few minutes
  • Release locks immediately when done
~ TMC orchestration log 
[TMC] agent-A registered: feat/auth
[TMC] agent-B registered: fix/ci-timeout
[TMC] agent-A locked: src/auth/login.ts
[TMC] agent-B requested: src/auth/login.ts
[TMC] agent-B: file locked — waiting
[TMC] agent-A released: src/auth/login.ts
[TMC] agent-B acquired: src/auth/login.ts
🔗
Killer Feature

Spec-Driven Traceability

When 20 agents are building across 20 projects simultaneously, you need to know exactly what was built, why it was built, and where to find the tests that prove it works. Spec IDs are the answer.

Every spec section gets a unique ID: [SPEC-001], [FEAT-AUTH-01], [REQ-003]. These IDs become the connective tissue of the entire system:

  • Spec sections define requirements: [SPEC-001] User Authentication
  • Code declares what it implements: // Implements [SPEC-001]
  • Tests declare what they verify: // Tests for [SPEC-001]
  • PRs list the spec IDs they address in the description
  • Plans reference spec IDs in their TODO checklists

The enforcement skill can audit the entire codebase and answer:

  • "What tests cover SPEC-001?" → grep for the ID
  • "Is SPEC-003 implemented?" → check for code references
  • "What's untested?" → find spec IDs with no test references
  • "What did this PR deliver?" → list all spec IDs in the diff
  • "Are there orphaned IDs?" → spec IDs nothing implements
  • "Is there unlinked code?" → code with no spec reference
Traceability isn't paperwork. It's how you stay sane at scale. Nothing ships without a reason. Nothing is untested without you knowing. Every line of code traces back to a requirement. Every test proves a requirement works.
~ SPEC-001 traceability matrix 
SPEC-001 User Authentication

implements:
  src/auth/login.ts
  src/auth/session.ts
  src/middleware/auth.ts

verifies:
  tests/auth/login.spec.ts
  tests/e2e/auth-flow.spec.ts

delivers:
  PR #42  · merged · CI green

scheduled:
  PLAN.md § Phase 2

───────────────────────────────
$ make audit-traceability
✓ All specs have IDs
✓ All specs have tests
✓ No orphaned IDs
✓ No unlinked code
Before & After

Serialised vs. parallel

What changes when you adopt Agent PMO.

Aspect Without Agent PMO With Agent PMO
Projects active at once 1 20+
Cross-project visibility Manual context-switching Dashboard refresh every 3 min
Agent setup per repo Custom per project Zero — standards enforced
Code quality on arrival Varies — you check manually All gates passed before you see it
Coverage regression Possible — no enforcement Impossible — monotonic threshold
Monorepo parallelism One agent, one branch Multiple worktrees, no conflicts
Cross-agent coordination None — agents collide TMC locks, sequences, coordinates
Your role Spectator waiting for agents Engineering director setting direction

See all of this in action.

Set up Agent PMO in minutes. One command. Open source and free.
